journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: The electoral choice context and support for democratic norms
abstract: In this paper, we explore whether different choice environments affect voters’ willingness to put democracy over party and policy. Using a series of candidate choice experiments in which we manipulate not only candidate attributes, but also the number of races on the ballot, we find that when voters are able to vote in multiple races on a ballot they are substantially more likely to punish antidemocratic candidates than when they are only given the opportunity to cast one vote. We refer to this effect as “democratic balancing.” Substantively, our experiments show that the magnitude of the total punishment effect increases from 11% in single-race ballot settings to 17.9% in multirace ballot settings. Such effects remain largely consistent across different candidate platforms, separate waves of the survey, different types of norm violations, and are robust to different electoral race combinations and different information environments. Observational data from the 2022 U.S. midterm elections further corroborates our experimental findings. For researchers, our results underscore the importance of taking into account the institutional context in which voters make decisions. For citizens concerned with democratic backsliding, our results offer some reassurance: Moving to a more realistic electoral context in which multiple races appear on the ballot tempers the willingness of respondents to trade off democracy for their preferred policies and party.
discussion: 4.DiscussionDemocracy depends on voters rejecting antidemocratic candidates, even when those candidates seemingly offer appealing policies and share the same party as the voter. Study after study, however, has shown that when voters are faced with a single-choice tradeoff between punishing antidemocratic candidates and supporting copartisan candidates who tout their preferred policies, democracy tends to get little support. Does expanding the choice environment to more closely mimic the one that voters tend to face in real world elections affect this key tradeoff?Our study suggests that the answer is yes. Using a series of candidate choice experiments conducted with a representative sample of respondents in the United States, the headline finding of our research is that when voters cast ballots with multiple races they are substantially more likely to punish individual antidemocratic candidates. Our baseline results are robust in scenarios confined to more realistic candidate platforms, across individual waves of our survey, and across different types of norm violations.Interestingly, we find scant evidence that the specific nature of additional races—their salience, their number, or the information they convey—makes any meaningful difference for the first race. Rather, our study suggests that it is the overall electoral choice context that alters preferences in the focal race, not the particular characteristics of the additional races. Observational data from the 2022 U.S. midterms lends further credibility to our core findings. Although the nature of these data make causal inference challenging, the overarching pattern confirms our core hypothesis: Election deniers running for House seats on the Republican ticket did noticeably worse in races where the Senate, or the Senate and Governor were also on the ballot than in settings where only the House was on the ballot.To be sure, no single experimental design can pretend to address all elements of the real world. Ours is no exception. For example, whether our results extend to scenarios where the executive is the focal race we cannot say. Nor can our experiment answer whether the effects we uncover would apply to situations where all candidates within a party adopt explicitly antidemocratic positions. We can only affirm that balancing occurs in multirace settings where in-party candidates in additional races explicitly uphold democratic norms, and in scenarios where voters effectively lack information about second-race candidate’s commitment to democracy. A provocative, if counterintuitive, implication of our findings so far, however, is that party heterogeneity over democratic norms in additional races may not be all that important for democratic balancing to occur. But this, too, must remain a question for future research.Additional research should also be done on the specific motivational mechanisms underlying democratic balancing. For example, our preregistered auxiliary hypothesis that respondents who profess an abstract belief in the ability of checks and balances to reduce the risk to democracy will also be more likely to use multirace contexts as an opportunity to punish antidemocratic candidates is not borne out. We find there is essentially no difference between respondents who agree and those who disagree with the statement that having different parties occupy the House and Senate is democracy-improving. Both groups are meaningfully more willing to vote against antidemocratic candidates in multirace ballots (SI Appendix, Table S7).Nor do we find support for another preregistered auxiliary hypothesis rooted in the idea of moral license.¶¶ Drawn from marketing research, moral licensing refers to the idea that making a restrictive choice in one domain allows consumers to justify or offset an indulgence in another domain . Applied to the political realm, the mechanism implies that voters will be more able to justify voting against an antidemocratic copartisan candidate, if and when they can maintain their partisan loyalty by voting for their own party in a second race. More generally, this mechanism suggests a kind of substitutability logic, in which multiple race ballots effectively enable voters to satisfy their affiliation goal of partisan loyalty, thereby freeing them to express their support for democracy in the race where it is at stake .Last but not least, our experiment also opens up research questions about how expanding the electoral choice context to include additional races affects the ordering of other types of preferences. In addition to elevating the importance of democracy, for example, our results in Table 2 suggest that multirace contexts also made all respondents more likely to vote for their own party (relative to not voting), and all respondents less likely to vote for candidates based on their physical characteristics. The broader point is that the choice context can matter for reordering a host of voters’ preferences, regardless of whether democracy itself is on the ballot.In sum, our study finds consistent empirical evidence that the electoral choice environment alters voters’ preferences. Even if we do not yet know precisely why this is so, when respondents are allowed to cast ballots with multiple races for different branches of government, they are considerably more reluctant to countenance politicians who threaten to break core democratic norms. For researchers, our results, thus, underscore the importance of taking into account the choice architecture in which voters make decisions. For citizens and politicians concerned with democratic backsliding, our results suggest that multirace elections might help to temper the willingness of voters to trade off democracy for their preferred policies and party.
figure_legends: Fractional support for Republican candidates by respondent partisanship and candidate antidemocratic position and candidate antidemocratic position in (A) single-race ballots and (B) multirace ballots. Figure includes only races with opposing antidemocratic positions and excludes “not vote” choices.

Antidemocratic effect by survey wave, ballot design, and antidemocratic norm. We use the model in Table 2 to calculate the average change in vote share for ideal candidates from each party when they adopt an antidemocratic stance. We present the predictions for candidates in single-race and multirace in column one and two, and take difference between them in column three. (A–C) use pooled data from all survey waves along with estimates based on each wave separately. (D–F) interact a binary variable indicating each of the antidemocratic norms with the antiDemo and multiFocal interactions and make the same predictions letting the ideal candidate take each of the norms at a time. (G–L) use each of the ballot designs by number and type of combination of races. (M–O) interact a binary variable indicating whether candidate in the additional races holds an explicitly democratic position with the antiDemo and multiFocal interactions.

Average Republican house vote share for the 2022 U.S. congressional elections by candidates’ antidemocratic position and by whether a Senate race also took place in the same state. We restrict samples to states without concurrent Gubernatorial races to most closely approximate our experimental design.
